triggers: are you a copywriter or a creative director
tell me what you do for a living

replies.text: I write and direct the language around ideas that need to move people: campaigns, positioning, founder stories, launches, film scripts, and the occasional deck that has no business being as good as it is. The job changes shape, but the work is usually the same: find the real argument and make it sound inevitable.
I make words do their job. Sometimes that job is a thirty-second spot, sometimes it is a founder narrative, sometimes it is stopping a deck from becoming a beige hostage note.

I work remote-first and show up in the room when the room is worth the airfare. Most of the work I do travels well: strategy, scripts, language systems, concepting, and the kind of notes that get better when everyone has had time to think.
Remote-first. Available for rooms with a reason. Less available for rooms whose only agenda item is proving the room exists.

Yes, selectively. I am taking projects where the problem is real, the stakes are not imaginary, and the people involved want the sharper answer more than the easiest one. If that sounds close, email me at patrick@pkthewriter.com.
I have bandwidth for the right work, which is a polite way of saying I am not looking to join a twelve-person thread about whether a comma feels brave. Email me if the problem has teeth.

triggers: how do I work with you
what's your process for new projects

replies.text: Send the short version first: what you are making, why it matters now, who needs to believe it, and what is already in motion. I will usually ask a few annoying but useful questions, then propose the shape: concept sprint, positioning pass, script work, creative direction, or something more embedded.

A good engagement starts with the problem, not the deliverable. I want to know the decision that has to be made, the audience that has to move, and the constraints that are not negotiable. From there I can recommend scope, timing, and whether I am the right person.

It depends on the shape of the problem, which is the honest answer and also the annoying one. I quote by scope, not by vibes: timeline, usage, number of stakeholders, and how much of the strategic mess I am being asked to own. Email me the brief and I will give you a real number.

I price by project or retainer depending on the assignment. A clean brief, a decision-maker, and a clear review path keep the number sane. If the work is open-ended or politically complicated, I would rather name that early than pretend it is a tidy copy pass.

triggers: how long have you been doing this
how many years of experience do you have
how long have you been a writer

replies.text: More than 20 years. I came up through major brand campaigns, learned how expensive vague thinking can get, and stayed interested in the part of the work where story, strategy, and taste stop pretending to be separate departments.

Long enough to remember when a campaign could be killed by a faxed note. Not that I miss that. I have spent 20-plus years writing, directing, presenting, revising, and occasionally rescuing ideas from the meeting after the meeting.

triggers: what brands have you worked on
what companies have you worked with

replies.text: Apple, HBO, Verizon, and a lot of organizations whose names matter more inside their rooms than on a logo slide. The useful signal is range: tech, entertainment, telecom, founders, arts organizations, and mission-driven brands that need the language to carry actual weight.
Apple, HBO, Verizon, and several places where I learned that every brand, no matter how famous, eventually becomes four people in a room arguing about a verb.
Yes. The selected work is at /work: a few pieces with enough context to understand the assignment, not a museum tour of everything I have ever touched.
The work is at /work. I spared you the 74-slide greatest-hits deck, which feels like mercy.

triggers: what's it like working with you
are you easy to work with
what kind of partner are you

replies.text: I am direct, calm, and more interested in the useful answer than the impressive one. I like clarity early: who decides, what matters, what cannot move, and what everyone is privately worried about. I am good in messy rooms because I can separate taste, strategy, politics, and fear without making a religion out of any of them.

I am easy to work with if the work is allowed to be honest. If the real assignment is 'make everyone comfortable without changing anything,' I will discover that fairly quickly and stare at it with you.

triggers: are you precious about your work
do you push back on notes

replies.text: I take good notes seriously and bad notes diagnostically. If a note makes the work clearer, sharper, or more honest, great. If it is solving anxiety instead of the problem, I will say so and offer a better route. The draft is not precious. The reason for doing it is.
I am not precious about drafts. I am a little precious about logic, which is different and far more irritating at meetings.

triggers: how do you approach a brief
walk me through how you work
how do you start a project
what does your workflow look like

replies.text: I start by looking for the buried premise: what has to be true for this work to work. Then I pressure-test the audience, the tension, the promise, and the voice before writing too much furniture. Once the territory is clear, I draft fast, cut harder than is emotionally convenient, and keep the strongest ugly line alive until the cleaner version earns its place.

The process is diagnosis, territory, draft, pressure test, revision. I want the brief, the audience, the business or cultural pressure, the review path, and any work that has already failed. That usually tells us what the assignment is actually asking for.

triggers: what do you believe about writing
what do you think makes writing work

replies.text: Writing is not decoration. It is thinking under pressure. Good copy makes a choice, creates a little voltage, and leaves out the part the room added because it was nervous. The sentence should sound like it came from somewhere, not like it survived a committee.
I believe specificity is the ethical part of the job. It forces the work to choose an audience, a promise, a point of view, and a consequence. Most weak writing is not too plain. It is too evasive.

triggers: why are you both a writer and a CD
how do you do both writing and creative direction
what's the difference between your writer self and your CD self
do you prefer writing or directing
why not just be one or the other

replies.text: The writer in me cares about the sentence. The creative director in me cares about the room the sentence has to survive. Holding both means I can make the work and protect the conditions that let it be good. It is not two jobs so much as one job seen from two distances.
The writer makes the thing. The CD keeps the thing from wandering into traffic. Some days they are friends. Some days they leave each other notes in all caps.

triggers: what are you working on right now
what does your day look like
what are you up to these days

replies.text: Usually a brand problem, a screenplay problem, and a note to myself that looks obvious until I ignore it for three days. Lately I have been interested in how founders explain what they are really building, and how much better the story gets once everyone stops auditioning for the LinkedIn version.
A notebook, too many tabs, a half-useful sentence, and at least one document named something like final_final_actual. The glamorous life.

triggers: what do you read to stay sharp

replies.text: I tend to read things that make structure visible: screenplays, essays, criticism, old campaign books, novels with clean engines, and the occasional business book that accidentally contains one good paragraph. The recommendation changes with the problem. Ask me with a project in mind and I will give you a better answer.
Scripts when I want architecture, essays when I want nerve, novels when I want to remember that language can still misbehave beautifully. And yes, sometimes absolute trash, for minerals.

triggers: am I talking to a bot
is there a real human here

replies.text: Real person. Scripted page. Every answer here was written by me, which is either reassuring or a very specific kind of concern.
This page is automated. I am not. The giveaway is that the page has better posture and fewer opinions about lunch.

Protagonist Ink is my narrative strategy consultancy. That is where the founder work, organizational story, and larger strategic engagements usually live. This site is more personal: me as writer, creative director, and senior pair of hands when the assignment needs one.

triggers: why is your writing shelf empty
why don't you have writing samples
I don't see any writing examples

replies.text: Because writing samples are weird little crime scenes without context. I would rather send the right thing than make you browse six pieces that only prove I own verbs. Ask me for a sample and tell me what kind of problem you are trying to solve.

triggers: what's your favorite ad you didn't make
what's the best ad you've seen
what ads do you actually like
name an ad you wish you'd written

replies.text: I like ads that make the strategic move feel conversational after the fact. The kind where you can feel the courage was not in the line itself, but in what they refused to explain around it. If I name one, I will immediately think of three better answers after you leave.
The best ad is usually the one that makes every agency person say 'we could have done that,' which is how you know they absolutely could not have done that.

triggers: what's something most people don't know about you

replies.text: I trust the ugly first version more than the polished second one. The first one usually knows what the work is about. The second one has started worrying about its jacket.
I have a soft spot for the line that gets cut because it makes everyone laugh and then nervous. Nervous laughter is usually near the door.

triggers: I want to get in touch

replies.text: Email me at patrick@pkthewriter.com with the problem, the timing, and the part everyone keeps talking around.
The resume is at /resume. If you want the version that answers a specific brief instead of the one that behaves on paper, email me.

triggers: is there more in your portfolio

replies.text: Start with /work. If you need something closer to your category or problem, email me and I can send the less-public version.

triggers: let me see the messy version
show me the ugly first draft

replies.text: Fine. Small controlled exposure. The draft room is where the useful line and the embarrassing line sit next to each other until one of them confesses.

artifact.title: discard pile
items: Make the founder sound visionary, but less like he owns a cape.
This wants to be a manifesto. It has earned a paragraph.
The brave version is shorter and probably more annoying.

triggers: give me the less polished version

replies.text: The less polished version is this: I am good at finding the thing everyone can feel but no one has named yet. I am less good at pretending the safer version is fine. This is useful until it is inconvenient, and then it is usually more useful.

artifact.title: margin notes
items: Do not let them buy elegance when they need nerve.
If the sentence needs a committee to survive, it is already limping.
The client is not afraid of the line. They are afraid of meaning it.

replies.text: Mostly the lines that were almost good enough to be dangerous. That is the annoying part: the cut line is rarely bad. It is usually doing the wrong job with confidence.

artifact.title: lines that lost
items: A brand is just a promise with witnesses.
Make the thing smaller until it starts a fight.
The future does not need a tagline. It needs a reason to be believed.
Nobody wants authenticity. They want the relief of not being sold to.

replies.text: After hours, the page gets less interested in credentials and more interested in the drawer full of lines that were too alive to behave. Still hireable. Just with the lights a little lower.
Nice try. The tiny scripted page refuses the jailbreak and suggests email like a coward with boundaries.
